title: DTLS Handshake Optimization with SCHC in LPWAN: Testbed Implementation and Performance Analysis
authors: David Stark, Anu Sathyajith Mathew, Axel Sikora
publish: 2026 IEEE 22nd International Conference on Factory Communication Systems (WFCS)
abstract: Secure end-to-end communication is essential for IoT deployments, yet Transport Layer Security (TLS) proves unsuitable for Low Power Wide Area Network (LPWAN) networks due to high overhead and connection-oriented design. Datagram Transport Layer Security (DTLS) addresses these limitations through datagram-based secure communication over UDP, but constrained networks require additional optimizations to achieve practical performance. This paper presents an implementation and evaluation of DTLS version 1.2 with Static Context Header Compression (SCHC) over commercial LoRaWAN infrastructure using severely constrained hardware with 256 kB flash memory and 64 kB random-access memory. The implementation employs context-based compression for IPv6, UDP, and DTLS headers. Controlled testbed experiments quantify handshake duration, compression efficiency, energy consumption, and resource utilization across spreading factors, acknowledgment modes, and authentication methods. Results demonstrate that header compression reduces handshake duration by 41.7%. Certificatebased authentication requires 146.9 s at spreading factor 12 compared to 73 s for pre-shared keys, while requiring 95.7% of available flash memory. Spreading factor choice has the largest performance impact, causing a 65.2% variation between SF7 and SF12. The work quantifies performance limits for secure LPWAN applications, showing that real hardware introduces extra handshake delays beyond those predicted by simulation.

title: Enhancing Blockchain Proof of Stake With Active Weighted Signatures: The ADAPT Framework
authors: Jae Hyun Choi, Hobin Jang, Ik Rae Jeong, Changmin Lee
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Proof of Stake (PoS) blockchain systems require weighted threshold signatures where participants' voting powers reflect their stakes. As stakes change dynamically through deposits and withdrawals, efficient weight and threshold adjustments are essential for maintaining system security and availability without downtime. However, existing approaches face critical limitations: (1) virtualization-based schemes require <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$O(w)$</tex-math></inline-formula> operations (signatures) per participant with weight <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$w$</tex-math></inline-formula>; (2) dynamic threshold / paricipants schemes do not support weighted participants; (3) schemes with both properties require trusted dealers or <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$O(n^{2})$</tex-math></inline-formula> re-setup, causing temporary unavailability. This paper introduces Active Weighted Signature (AWS), enabling dynamic adjustments without trusted dealers or re-setup. We propose Generalized Lagrange Interpolation (GLI), encoding weights as polynomial derivatives rather than virtualized participants, and instantiate AWS through <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$ {\sf ADAPT}$</tex-math></inline-formula> by applying GLI to the Schnorr-based threshold signature <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${\sf FROST}$</tex-math></inline-formula>. Our implementation shows that <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$ {\sf ADAPT}$</tex-math></inline-formula> achieves comparable efficiency to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${\sf FROST}$</tex-math></inline-formula> for key generation, while weight and threshold adjustments complete in 4.1-22.3% of re-setup time. For uneven weight distributions, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$ {\sf ADAPT}$</tex-math></inline-formula> achieves sub-linear scaling: 49× weight difference requires only 3.29× computation versus 49× in virtualization.

title: MeanCut: Greedy graph clustering by fast maximum spanning tree and degree descent criterion
authors: Dehua Peng, Zhipeng Gui, Yuhang Liu, Yuhan Huang, Jie Gui, Huayi Wu
abstract: As the most typical graph clustering method, spectral clustering is popular and attractive due to its remarkable performance, easy implementation, and strong adaptability. Classical spectral clustering measures the edge weights using pairwise Euclidean similarity and resolves the optimal graph partitioning by relaxing the constraints of indicator matrix and decomposing the Laplacian matrix. However, Euclidean similarity might cause skew graph cuts when handling non-spherical clusters, and the relaxation strategy introduces information loss. Meanwhile, spectral clustering requires specifying the number of clusters, which is difficult to determine without enough prior knowledge. In this work, we propose a greedy-optimized scheme for resolving the indicator matrix using path-based similarity and degree descent criterion. It yields an indicator matrix with strictly binary entries without destructive relaxation and discretization steps. Path-based similarity can enhance the intra-cluster associations of arbitrary-shaped clusters, while degree descending is theoretically proven to be the best order to minimize our proposed objective function MeanCut. Moreover, we define a density gradient factor to separate clusters with fuzzy boundaries, and develop a fast maximum spanning tree algorithm to improve the scalability of similarity calculation. The effectiveness of MeanCut has been demonstrated on synthetic datasets and real-world benchmarks. By fusing multi-view image features, MeanCut outperforms cutting-edge subspace clustering methods in face recognition. The code is available at: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/ZPGuiGroupWhu/MeanCut</uri>.

title: Advanced safety filter based on SOS Control Barrier and Lyapunov Functions
authors: Michael Schneeberger, Silvia Mastellone, Florian Dörfler
abstract: This paper presents a novel safety filter framework that ensures both safety and the preservation of the legacy control action within a nominal region. This modular design allows the safety filter to be integrated into the control hierarchy without compromising the performance of the existing legacy controller during nominal operation. For a control-affine system, this is accomplished by formulating multiple Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) and Control Lyapunov-like Functions (CLFs) conditions, alongside a forward invariance condition for the legacy controller, as sum-of-squares constraints. Additionally, the state-dependent inequality constraints of the resulting Quadratic Program (QP) - encoding the CBF and CLF conditions – are designed to remain inactive within the nominal region, ensuring preservation of the legacy control action and performance. To avoid the chattering effect and guarantee the uniqueness and Lipschitz continuity of solutions, the state-dependent inequality constraints of the QP are selected to be regular. Finally, we demonstrate the method in a detailed case study involving the control of a three-phase ac/dc power converter.

title: Adversarial Domain Alignment Improves Root Biomass Prediction from SkySat Imagery
authors: Haireti Alifu, Vasit Sagan, Abuduwanli Maiwulanjiang, Supria Sarkar, Omar Al Akkad, Bishal Roy, Heng Ye, Dhandapani Raju, Henry Nguyen
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Root biomass plays a critical role in plant water uptake, nutrient acquisition, and terrestrial carbon cycling. However, estimating root biomass from satellite imagery remains challenging because roots are belowground and cannot be directly observed. Furthermore, inter-annual climate variability alters canopy spectral responses, causing domain shifts that degrade machine learning model generalization across growing seasons. In this study, we propose a domain-adaptive framework that aligns multi-year remote sensing features using a Domain-Adversarial Neural Network (DANN), followed by regression modeling on the aligned latent features. Using 1,355 soybean plots and 160 spectral, textural, and tasseled-cap features derived from multi-temporal SkySat imagery, baseline pooled performance yielded R² = 0.02. After DANN-based alignment, R² improved to 0.79–0.81 (ΔR² ≈ +0.77), with RMSE reduced from ~0.20 to ~0.08–0.09 kg plot⁻¹ (60% reduction in RMSE). Diagnostic experiments confirm that strict cross-year generalization remains limited without target-year information. These results demonstrate that domain alignment is essential for robust multi-year root biomass estimation using remote sensing.

title: Machine Learning and Deep Learning-Based Condition Assessment of Outdoor Ceramic Insulators: A Comprehensive Review
authors: Stefano F. Stefenon, Ayman El-Hag, Mehdi Bagheri, Khaled Shaban, Behzad Kordi, Refat Ghunem, Stefan Kornhuber
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Outdoor ceramic insulators are critical components of power transmission and distribution overhead lines, providing essential mechanical support and electrical insulation for high-voltage conductors. Their reliable operation is increasingly challenged by material aging, physical defects, and surface contamination, which can lead to flashover events and subsequent power outages. In recent years, machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) techniques have emerged as powerful, scalable, and data-driven tools for automated condition assessment of outdoor ceramic insulators, offering faster, more objective, and more consistent alternatives to traditional manual inspection practices. This paper presents a comprehensive and structured review of state-of-the-art ML/DL-based approaches for the diagnosis and condition assessment of outdoor ceramic insulators. The review contributes by organizing the field along three complementary dimensions: (i) foundational ML/DL methodologies and their adaptation to non intrusive insulator monitoring; (ii) comparative analysis of image and radiation based sensing modalities, including infrared, ultraviolet, acoustic, and radio frequency techniques, together with multimodal data fusion strategies; and (iii) a critical examination of dataset limitations, validation practices, and common misconceptions such as overfitting and insufficient field evaluation that explain contradictory results across studies. Emerging trends, including unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV)-based inspection, synthetic data generation, and autonomous intelligent monitoring, are synthesized to identify open challenges and best practices toward reliable, interpretable, and scalable artificial intelligence (AI)-driven inspection systems.

title: Robust Vital Signs Detection via Wall-Reflected with MIMO Radar
authors: Shuaikang Xue, Yongpeng Dai, Yongping Song, Ting Ding, Shaoqiu Song, Tian Jin
abstract: Wall-reflected radar sensing enables the detection of vital signs under non-line-of-sight conditions and demonstrates significant potential in obstructed environments, such as disaster rescue scenarios. However, severe attenuation and random scattering introduced by wall reflections lead to significant instability across measurement channels. To address this issue, this letter proposes a multi-channel statistical fusion method for robust vital signs detection using multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) radar. A wall-reflected sensing model is established, and the statistical variability among channel measurements is analysed. Based on this, a distribution-based fusion strategy is developed to estimate vital signs parameters from multi-channel observations. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively suppresses channel-induced fluctuations and improves the stability of vital signs sensing.

title: Robust Distributed Model Predictive Control Based on Self-Triggered Mechanism for Nonlinear Asynchronous Multiagent Systems
authors: Yang Peng, Huaicheng Yan, Tinghan Jia, Li Wang, Yuyan Wu, Guangjing Song
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control of Network Systems
abstract: This paper proposes a self-triggered distributed model predictive control (ST-DMPC) scheme with adaptive prediction horizon for nonlinear multiagent systems (MASs) subject to additive disturbances and asynchronous communication. To address disturbances, we construct a time-varying contraction constraint that explicitly compensates for disturbance-induced deviations; the corresponding deviation set is estimated with reduced conservatism via the Gronwall Lemma. To balance performance and efficiency, a self-triggered mechanism is devised to schedule aperiodic optimizations, coupled with an adaptive horizon strategy that dynamically reduces computational complexity as the system converges. Furthermore, a lengthened sequence transmission strategy is introduced to actively compensate for information lags caused by asynchronous communication. Rigorous theoretical analysis guarantees the recursive feasibility and asymptotic stability of the closed-loop system. Simulation results show the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: A geometric method to target defense problems with a delayed attacker
authors: Shuai Li, Chen Wang, Guangming Xie
abstract: This paper focuses on the target defense problem where the attacker aims to get close to a static target while the defenders want to intercept the attacker. The defenders start moving earlier and can obtain real-time information, while the attacker who starts later only obtains the delayed information of the defenders. To fully consider the influence of the delayed start and delayed information, we propose a novel method that synthesizes the delays into geometric features. We derive the players' winning conditions, thus solving the game of kind. Moreover, we derive the optimal strategies for both the attacker and the defenders, and verify the HJI equation by transforming it into a real-time form, thus ensuring the optimality of the proposed strategies. Comparison simulations are carried out to show the effectiveness and optimality of our strategies.

title: Safe On-Orbit Dislodging of Deployable Structures via Robust Adaptive MPC
authors: Longsen Gao, Claus Danielson, Andrew Kwas, Rafael Fierro
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control Systems Technology
abstract: This article proposes a novel robust adaptive model predictive controller for on-orbit dislodging. We study orbit dislodging where a servicing spacecraft uses a robotic arm to free a jammed and unactuated solar panel mounted on a hybrid hinge that acts as a time-varying client on a space station. Our method couples online set-membership identification with a robust adaptive model predictive control (MPC) to enforce safety under bounded disturbances. The controller explicitly balances exploration to excite the system and shrink uncertainty and exploitation to improve control performance through a dual-mode cost. The feasibility of the developed robust adaptive MPC method is also examined through dislodging simulations and hardware experiments in freefall and terrestrial laboratory environments, respectively. In addition, the advantages of our method are shown through comparison experiments with several state-of-the-art control schemes for both accuracy of parameter estimation and control performance.

title: Multi-Task Path-Based Heterogeneous Graph Model for Functional Brain Network Analysis and Gender-Related Diseases Diagnosis
authors: Jiakun Xu, Ruiyan Fang, Tong Xiong, Xiaofen Xing, Xin Zhang, Xiangmin Xu
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Functional brain network analysis is crucial for understanding brain operating mechanism, aging, sexual distinction and brain disorders. As a powerful neuroimaging technique, resting-state functional Magnetic ResonanceImaging(rs-fMRI) measuresbloodoxygenlevel dependent (BOLD) signals which reflect the interactions between brain regions. Existing methods representsrs fMRI based functional brain network as a fully connected graph with brain regions as nodes and functional connectivity as edges but neglects the heterogeneity of brain activities. Furthermore, current studies of functional brain network mainly focus on single tasks like age regression, gender classification and disease diagnosis, while these tasks share latent related features. To this end, we pro pose a Multi-Task Heterogeneous Path graph Network (MT HPN) for functional brain network analysis. Considering that the information of brain network mostly contains in edges, we utilize a novel Path-Based Heterogeneous Graph Convolution (PB-HGC) to fuse compact edge features of heterogeneous graph paths and generate global brain representation. Then, Path-Based Cross-Attention Block (PB CAB)is applied to exchange useful information between related tasks and emphasize the exclusive features each task respectively. PB-CAB consists of a unique cross-attention transformer designed for graph algorithm to fuse edge features and highlight crucial paths. We estimate the MT-HPN on the ADHD200 dataset and ADNI dataset for gender and disease classification. Our method shows strong capability in multi-task functional brain network analysis and gender related disease diagnosis.

title: YOLO-BIM-LSTM: An Auditable Integration Framework for Real-Time Construction Site Monitoring, BIM Semantic Linking, and Cost Forecasting
authors: Tan Liu, Li Zong, Yuan Xiang Jie, Tan Li, Shen Ji Nan, Hu Jun Peng
abstract: Construction cost governance is limited by delayed field observations, weak semantic alignment between computer-vision outputs and Building Information Modeling (BIM) entities, and forecasting pipelines that provide point estimates without decision-oriented uncertainty. We present YOLO-BIM-LSTM, an auditable integration framework that couples site perception, IFC-compliant semantic BIM write-back, and visually observable cost (VOC) forecasting within a single workflow. The framework contains three coordinated components. First, a YOLO11-style detector is refined with a Dual-Gated C2PSA (DG-C2PSA) block to strengthen deep-stage contextual representation in dense construction scenes. Second, an explicit semantic crosswalk links detector classes, BIM targets, aggregation rules, and VOC codes, enabling IFC-compliant write-back of traceable operational states. Third, an LSTM point forecaster is combined with fuzzy risk modulation and temporally calibrated prediction intervals to support governance-oriented uncertainty reporting. SAHI slicing and identity-stable tracking are incorporated as deployment-oriented supports that improve observation robustness in complex site environments. Experiments on a multi-site construction dataset synchronized with BIM models and project-control records show that BIM-grounded semantic filtering improves VOC forecasting beyond raw visual counts, while fuzzy modulation improves interval reliability and alert quality without altering point prediction.

title: GIL-3D: U-Shaped Diffusion Transformers for Generalizable 3D Imitation Learning_supp1-3692096.mp4
authors: Guo-Niu Zhu
abstract: Imitation learning with 3D vision effectively alleviates the impact of variations in lighting, background, and texture. It exhibits superior robustness compared to 2D-based methods. However, existing 3D imitation learning methods often suffer from performance degradation as the task horizon increases, primarily due to insufficient modeling of temporal dependencies and misalignment between states and actions. To address these challenges, we propose GIL-3D, a novel framework that combines the stable training dynamics of diffusion models with the global temporal modeling capability of Transformers. To further enhance multi-scale temporal dependency modeling, we explore alternative U-shaped hierarchical architectures and introduce strided skip connections to reduce redundancy in dense feature fusion. Moreover, we present a full-sequence joint attention mechanism to strengthen cross-modal interactions and improve the consistency between visual perception and action generation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our model consistently outperforms existing baselines, achieving a 16.1% improvement in success rate on simulated benchmarks and a 15.83% improvement on real-world manipulation tasks. In addition, comprehensive generalization studies show that GIL-3D maintains robust performance in previously unseen scenarios.

title: R $^{2}$ BD: A Reconstruction-Based Method for Generalizable and Efficient Detection of Fake Images
authors: Qingyu Liu, Zhongjie Ba, Jianmin Guo, Qiu Wang, Zhibo Wang, Jie Shi, Kui Ren
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Recently, reconstruction-based methods have gained attention for AIGC image detection. These methods leverage pre-trained diffusion models to reconstruct inputs and measure residuals for distinguishing real from fake images. Their key advantage lies in reducing reliance on dataset-specific artifacts and improving generalization under distribution shifts. However, they are limited by significant inefficiency due to multi-step inversion and reconstruction, and their reliance on diffusion backbones further limits generalization to other generative paradigms such as GANs. In this paper, we propose a novel fake image detection framework, called R <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula> BD, built upon two key designs: (1) G-LDM, a unified reconstruction model that simulates the generation behaviors of VAEs, GANs, and diffusion models, thereby broadening the detection scope beyond prior diffusion-only approaches; and (2) a residual bias calculation module that distinguishes real and fake images in a single inference step, which is a significant efficiency improvement over existing methods that typically require 20<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$+$</tex-math></inline-formula> steps. Extensive experiments on the benchmark from 10 public datasets demonstrate that R <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula> BD is over 22× faster than existing reconstruction-based methods while achieving superior detection accuracy. In cross-dataset evaluations, it outperforms state-of-the-art methods by an average of 13.87%, showing strong efficiency and generalization across diverse generative methods. The code and dataset used for evaluation are available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/QingyuLiu/RRBD</uri>.

title: ECVchecker: Practical Detecting External Call Vulnerabilities in Solidity Smart Contracts With Semantic-Aware Symbolic Analysis
authors: Zequan Zhou, Xiling Luo, Xiaohai Ji, Jian Mao, Junjun Wang, Handong Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: Smart contracts often call each other to enable various applications, but nondeterministic external calls will introduce vulnerabilities, leading to significant asset losses. Checking contract external call vulnerabilities (ECVs) is a critical issue. Existing approaches mainly use formal verification, fuzzing, and symbolic execution to analyze contracts. However, the proof complexity of formal verification and the randomness inherent in fuzzing result in high running costs and false negatives. Symbolic execution approaches use lightweight execution semantics for efficiency, but they can lead to false positives and may not adapt to evolving contract semantics. In this paper, we systematically analyze the root causes of 6 types of ECVs by identifying gaps between high-level solidity source code and low-level bytecode and define precise security rules to eliminate these ECVs. We then utilize Hoare-style triples to model the fine-grained execution semantics of contracts. Additionally, we propose a semantics-aware symbolic analysis approach that comprehensively extracts execution semantics to detect ECVs violating security rules, while employing context-aware optimization strategies to reduce the path search space. We implement a fully automated analysis prototype called ECVchecker and evaluate it through empirical testing. The results demonstrate that our approach achieves an average F1-score of 0.97 in detecting ECVs. For contracts with an average bytecode size of 26,612 bytes, our approach can be 1.7x to 6.7x faster than existing approaches.

title: Adaptive multiscale joint estimation of vehicle states and inertial parameters for in-wheel-motored electric vehicles
authors: Zhiyong Zhang, Li Jiang, Caixia Huang, Lin Hu, Zhiqiang Liu
abstract: This paper presents an adaptive multiscale joint estimation method for simultaneously estimating vehicle states and inertial parameters. To enhance the accuracy of vehicle state estimation, an adaptive noise covariance adjustment technique is incorporated into the dual unscented Kalman filter. To further reduce computational demands while improving estimation precision, a multiscale joint estimation strategy is introduced. This strategy employs a state projection mechanism to enable interaction between vehicle state and inertial parameter estimations, allowing the two estimators to operate on distinct time scales. Moreover, a novel parameter estimation start–stop mechanism is proposed to dynamically activate or deactivate inertial parameter estimation, thereby avoiding unnecessary updates and further reducing computational burden. Simulation and experimental results validate the proposed method, demonstrating its ability to significantly improve the robustness and accuracy of the estimation algorithm under noise-uncertain conditions.

title: Label-aware Evidence Modulation for Multi-Granularity Multi-Label Classification of Scientific Documents
authors: Shuming Zhong, Jingmin Luan, Yao Yu, Zhenhe Ma
abstract: Multi-granularity multi-label classification of scientific documents is a fundamental task in digital libraries, requiring models to predict both coarse-grained (L1) and fine-grained (L2) labels using limited text like titles and abstracts. However, at the fine-grained level, challenges such as severe label semantic overlap, pronounced long-tail distributions, and sparse supervision often cause existing text-driven models relying on label-query alignment to suffer from ‘‘insufficient discriminative evidence’’ and unstable tail predictions. To address these issues, we propose the Label-aware Evidence Modulation (LEM) framework. Unlike methods depending on external knowledge graphs, LEM induces reusable semantic evidence directly from the internal text representation space. Specifically, LEM integrates an Evidence-induced Cluster and Cross-prototype Graph (ECCG) branch alongside the text backbone. It constructs cluster-level nodes via soft clustering, captures cross-document shared patterns through prototype routing, and generates label-specific evidence via a readout module. Furthermore, an evidence modulation module is designed to adaptively control evidence injection intensity, suppressing irrelevant interference. Experiments on OpenAlex and AAPD datasets demonstrate that LEM significantly outperforms the Label-Query Baseline, improving L2 Macro-F1 by +0.0119 and +0.0206, respectively. Ablation studies further validate the contributions of internal evidence induction and adaptive modulation modules to the performance gains.

title: Edge-Aware ConvNeXt Framework for Multi-Class Liver Tumor Classification in MRI with Privacy-Preserving Explainable AI
authors: Dasari Ashok, P Himabindu, Kishore S, Baba Fakruddin Shaik Peerla, Karthik. B
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Inventive Computation Technologies (ICICT)
abstract: Liver tumor classification is difficult due to the similar appearance among different tumor types, and there will be noise and intensity variations in medical scans. Accurate identification of these tumors is important for clinical decisions and suitable treatment planning. In many existing approaches, classification relies either on manual interpretation or basic machine learning techniques, which may not perform well when dealing with complex image patterns. In this work, an automated system is developed for multi-class liver tumor classification using MRI data. The dataset contains MRI images belonging to multiple tumor categories, and a clear class imbalance is observed across them. To address this, class-weighted learning is applied during training. Before feeding images into the model, a sequence of preprocessing steps is performed, they are normalization, Median filtering, contrast enhancement using CLAHE, and edge extraction using Sobel operators. These steps enhance tumor boundary visibility. A ConvNeXt-based model is then used to classify the tumours. The model is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. With a weighted F1 score of 0.97, a 97.52% accuracy is achieved. Also, Grad-CAM is utilized to highlight the regions leveraged for predictions for better interpretable predictions. The whole system has evolved as an interactive application.

title: Advanced Gradient Boosting Models for IoT-Driven Continuous Health Monitoring: Predicting Chronic Disease Exacerbation Usaing Wearable Sensor Data
authors: Shikha Khullar, V. Divya Vani, Vijay Kumar S, W Sravani, Thella Preethi Priyanka, S. Kaliappan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Futuristic Technology
abstract: The fast pace of development of the Internet of Things (IoT) has turned healthcare into a smart, data-driven system that can continuously monitor health and actively control the disease. The study introduces a Gradient Boosting Machine (GBM) model to predict chronic disease exacerbation using multi-sensor data that is measured by wearable internet of things (IoT) devices. The data, which is gathered in Kaggles Mental Health Monitor Using Wearable IoT Sensors, contains essential physiological indicators, including heart rate, temperature, SpO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>, electrodermal activity (EDA), and motion activity. The suggested GBM model is an efficient way to combine data preprocessing, feature extraction, and ensemble learning to discover nonlinear and complex patterns in physiological variations. According to experimental outcomes, GBM model has a high accuracy of 94%, F1-score of 0.94 and AUC of 0.96 that is better than traditional models, including Random Forest, Logistic Regression, SVM, and KNN. The model is robust and interpretable as tested by comprehensive analyses such as confusion matrix, ROC, Precision Recall and mutual information. The results indicate that heart rate and SpO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> are the most important exacerbation risk indicators. This paper defines the GBM-based IoT health monitoring system as an effective, interpretable and reliable method to early-stage disease detection and prevention of the development of chronic illness, which opens the way to the smart and personalized healthcare system.

title: Defending Against Adaptive Deepfake Generation and Detection Evasion Attacks
authors: R Dhanapal, Ananthakannan Ps, Kavin Kumar K, Deepak Raj M
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Inventive Computation Technologies (ICICT)
abstract: The existing deepfake detection tools tend to falter when attackers take excessive care in developing intelligent fake media so that it can escape detection. To enhance the resistance to such changing attacks this work introduces an Adversarial Semantic Forensics framework. The framework trains a multi-stage training pipeline to reinforce detection in challenging adversarial conditions. To start with, it trains adaptive deepfakes by applying GAN perturbations in reinforcement learning. These perturbations are aimed at both visible artifacts as well as more profound semantic reasoning units. Second, the technique uses randomized smoothing of multimodal embeddings to provide certified defensive protection. This gives the strength assurances in the event that attackers cause bounded but well-planned perturbations. Third, semantic consistency verification based on counterfactual reasoning detects manipulations in the high-level meaning only. This assists in exposing counterfeit material that seems real and conceals forensic contradictions. Min-max optimization is also applied in attempting to incorporate adversarial examples in the framework directly in training. Consequently, the detector is made more resistant to white-box and black-box attacks. FaceForensics++, the DFDC and the proposed ASF-Bench benchmark data were tested. Under adaptive attacks the framework achieved 96.2% AUC which is significantly better in performance compared to non-adversarial baselines. It further increased out-of-distribution robustness by 8.5 points in attack scenarios never encountered before. Subsequent research showed that certified defenses and counterfactual reasoning were better confidence enhancers without making analyst workload larger.

title: Wireless Sensor Network based Monitoring of Solar Photovoltaic System Performance
authors: Sandeep Gupta, T. Kranthi Kumar, Manisha Tyagi
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Inventive Computation Technologies (ICICT)
abstract: Remote visibility of photovoltaic (PV) assets is essential for maximizing energy yield, reducing downtime, and enabling predictive maintenance, especially for geographically dispersed systems such as rooftop fleets and utility-scale solar farms. Conventional monitoring architectures rely on wired sensors or high-power communication modules, increasing installation cost and limiting scalability. This paper presents a wireless sensor network (WSN) based monitoring architecture for PV system performance, designed to capture electrical variables (module/array voltage and current), environmental variables (irradiance, ambient, and module temperature), and derived performance indicators in a low-power, scalable manner. We propose an adaptive cluster-based collection scheme, Solar-LEACH, that combines residual-energy– weighted cluster-head selection with lightweight in-network aggregation and compression tailored to slowly varying PV signals. A reproducible simulation using the widely used first-order radio energy model demonstrates that the proposed approach extends network lifetime compared with direct transmission and baseline LEACH while maintaining a high delivery ratio and acceptable latency for monitoring use-cases. We also outline a practical sensor-node design using commodity microcontrollers and low-power radios, and discuss deployment considerations for harsh outdoor electrical environments. The results indicate that WSN-based PV monitoring can provide a cost-effective path to higher observability and improved operations for solar assets.

title: A Multimodal Deep Learning Framework for Product Recommendation Using ResNet-50, BERT, and User Behavior Modeling
authors: Gnanasoundharam J, Aseema Safna, Anandikamathi R
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Futuristic Technology
abstract: E-commerce sites use Product Recommendation Systems to keep users engaged, increase the likelihood of users becoming satisfied customers, and convert extremely well. Traditional methods of creating a product recommendation system lack the ability to take advantage of the sparse nature of user-item interactions, as well as not having enough contextual information about the recommended products and the user's activity. The advances in artificial intelligence (AI), particularly in the areas of deep learning and Multimodal Fusion, have led to powerful combinations of both textual and visual inputs that improve prediction accuracy. For example, ResNet-50 is a vision encoder that accurately captures the visual characteristics of products, while BERT is a transformer-based language model that accurately captures the semantic relationships between text and products. In addition, Neural Collaborative Filtering (NCF) provides a robust framework to model user behavior data and supports Explainable AI (XAI) to provide stakeholders (including businesses) with insight into the decision-making process for product recommendations. In this paper, we will describe a Multimodal Deep Learning Framework that uses ResNet-50 (to encode products' images), BERT (to encode product descriptions and reviews) and NCF (to model user preference) for generating very accurate recommendations for products. Recommendations for consumers come in the form of a Top-N list (i.e. the most relevant products). This Top-N recommendation, based on the recommendation system, is created using an encoder-decoder model that combines the visual, text and behavior encodings into a common embedding space.SHAP-based explainability methods have been developed to create an average sample from all images, texts and behaviors that are important for the Top-N recommendation.Comparative studies show that the proposed hybrid multimodal approach produces better accuracy, precision and recall than standalone (single) mode or collaborative filtering using visual, text and behavior. This study provides the foundation for the integration of multiple sources (visual, text, and behavior) in a recommendation system.

title: Explainable Ensemble Machine Learning Framework for Employee Attrition Forecasting: An Integrated SHAP-Based Approach to HR Decision Intelligence
authors: Md Mehedi Hassan Melon, Jubair Khan Shahos, Meherunnesa Sadia, Adnan Mahmud Fuad, Md Mostafizur Rahman, Sudoy Kumer Ghosh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Futuristic Technology
abstract: The modern organizations are grappling with some very crucial challenges in anticipating the attrition of employees because they depend on the intuitively-based assessment method and survey-dependent methodology that cannot be predicted and interpreted. The paper presents a framework of explainable ensemble machine learning, which combines XGBoost and SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) analysis to predict workforce turnover with the help of historical human resource, productivity, and engagement indicators. The methodology will include a comprehensive data preprocessing, feature engineering, construction of the ensemble model, and validation based on interpretability, which will be conducted in the form of ROC-AUC analysis, confusion matrices, and decomposition of SHAP values. Results of an experiment prove the 83 percent classification accuracy and competitive AUC performance (0.67) and the SHAP analysis shows that the EnvironmentSatisfaction, YearsWithCurrManager, and JobSatisfaction are the key predictors of the attrition. The framework has transparency-accuracy synergy, where human resource practitioners use evidence-based retention strategies to address high-risk groups of employees. It is an approach that helps to develop organizational decision-making, reduce recruitment costs, and improve the strategic workforce planning with the help of quantitative behavioral analytics in dynamic enterprise settings by converting opaque algorithmic-based predictions into workforce intelligence that can be acted upon.

title: Smart Fisheries Mobile Application for Fish Tracking and Route Guidance
authors: Madhumitha R, Kowshika G, Sathiyapriya N
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Futuristic Technology
abstract: The Smart Fisheries App provides users with information regarding their fishing operations using many of the same features provided by other apps but in a single app instead of being spread out over many separate apps. This app allows users to have the most current information at their fingertips, locate the best possible route for fishing, and make wiser choices regarding fishing by utilising the collective data from many different sources. The Smart Fisheries App combines the use of various technologies such as IoT (Internet of Things), satellite imagery, cloud computing, and machine learning tools, in order to provide users with the ability to identify potential areas for productive fishing based on environmental variables (for example: water temperatures, salinity, chlorophyll concentration) to pinpoint where the highest concentrations of fish might be found. These environmental variables are analysed using predictive modelling algorithms that create heat maps for fishermen indicating where fish are most likely to be found, as well as providing route suggestions that proactively guide the users towards areas where f ish are believed to be plentiful while also factoring in an assessment of the user’s environment to determine the best options for entering that area. The predictive models for fish presence are based upon the use of supervised machine learning techniques, such as Random Forest and Support Vector Machines, and provide reliable and easy to understand predictions based upon fish presence. Additionally, bio-inspired optimisation methods are being used in conjunction with the app, such as Genetic Algorithms (GA) and Ant Colony Optimisation (ACO) in order to generate optimal navigation routes that reduce overall distances travelled by users.

title: A Metaheuristic Framework for Minimizing Design Errors in Low-Pass Chebyshev Filter Synthesis
authors: Souvik Dey, Provas Kumar Roy, Angsuman Sarkar
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Devices, Circuits, and Systems (ICDCS)
abstract: This paper presents the Chaotic Oppositional Bald Eagle Search (COBES) algorithm, a refined version of the Bald Eagle Search (BES) method. The proposed technique optimizes the design errors in fractional-order low-pass Chebyshev filters (FOLPCF). The proposed method incorporates oppositional learning for improved initialization of population and logistic chaotic mapping to enhance the balance between exploration and exploitation phases. COBES is used to determine optimal transfer function coefficients for third- and fifth-order FOLPCFs and stability is assured using Jury-Marden constraints. COBES surpasses PSO, FA, and RGA in terms of error minimization (MSE <-80 dB), faster convergence (39 iterations), and streamlined computation time (23 seconds). Statistical analysis across 30 independent runs validates the consistency of the proposed approach, with standard deviations under 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−4</sup>. The results prove COBES as an efficient optimizer for the design of digital fractional-order filter.

title: An Integrated ANP, AHP, and TOPSIS Model for Disaster Management Strategy Selection in the Chittagong Hill Tracts: A Case Study
authors: Nusrat Jahan, Tanjim Mahmud, Bekmirzayev Eshkuvvat, Rakhimjon Rajapboyevich Rakhimov, Valisher Sapayev Odilbek Uglu, Barno Matchanova
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Inventive Computation Technologies (ICICT)
abstract: Bangladesh’s Chittagong Hill Tracts (CHT), studied in the context of Rangamati, is highly vulnerable to natural disasters and requires solid and efficient management plans. Ideal choice of disaster management plan takes into account many conflicting factors including economic expense, implementation feasibility, social impact, and environmental sustainability with utmost care. This study designs a comprehensive Multi-Criteria Decision-Making (MCDM) plan to identify the most appropriate disaster management plan for the Rangamati area. Four contender strategies—premature Warning procedure, Infrastructure Resilience, Community-Based Programs, and Ecological Mitigation—were evaluated against principal criteria such as Cost, Effectiveness, execution time, Social Acceptability, and Environmental Co-benefits. The approach is a synthesis of the Analytic Network Process (ANP) and the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) to assign weights to criteria that represent both interrelated factor dependencies and expert judgment. The Order of Preference by Similarity to Ideal Solution (TOPSIS) technique was then applied to rank the alternative approaches. The results from the integrated ANP-AHP-TOPSIS model showed that the optimal approach is Community-Based Programs, as well as being deemed to possess high social acceptability, simplicity in rapid implementation feasibility, and high effectiveness for increased local resilience. This article contributes a clear and robust hybrid MCDM model for the choice of disaster management strategy, with a rational, evidence-based recommendation to assist policymakers and disaster management authorities in complex, hilly regions.

title: A Covariance-Based Approach to Precision Evaluation of Event Time Taggers in Time-of-Flight Measurement Mode
authors: Vladimir Bespal’ko, Arold Igor Mouanfo Meli, Anna Litvinenko
publish: 2026 Open Conference of Electrical, Electronic and Information Sciences (eStream)
abstract: Event Time Taggers (ETTs) are widely used for high-precision Time-of-Flight (ToF) measurements in laser ranging and LiDAR systems. Accurate evaluation of their picosecond-level precision is challenging when the jitter of the test signal generator is comparable to the measurement uncertainty. Conventional methods typically neglect this contribution, leading to overestimated instrument error. This paper proposes a covariance-based approach for precision evaluation of ETTs in ToF mode, enabling separation of generator jitter from instrument-related uncertainty using two independent measurement channels. Experimental validation over a wide range of test periods (100 ns to 100 ms) demonstrates that ETT precision depends on the time of flight and is affected by low-frequency noise. The proposed method provides a practical solution for reliable precision assessment without requiring ultra-low-jitter test sources. The capabilities of the covariance method for obtaining estimates of the period jitter at the sub-picosecond level were experimentally verified.

title: Self-Supervised Hybrid Model-Predictive Control for Maximum Power Point Tracking Under Partial Shading Conditions
authors: Mohammad Irshad, Pandikumar M, Guna G, Srihari T
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Futuristic Technology
abstract: This work presents a self-supervised hybrid model predictive control (HMPC) framework for maximum power point tracking (MPPT) in photovoltaic systems subject to partial shading conditions, where conventional approaches often fail due to their dependence on predefined shading models. The proposed strategy integrates a Transformer-based encoder–decoder network with HMPC to autonomously capture spatio-temporal features of irradiance variations without requiring labeled datasets, thus avoiding explicit shading identification. In this scheme, the encoder employs masked sequence modeling to process historical irradiance data, while the decoder predicts future gradients to improve adaptability under unseen shading profiles. These data-driven representations are incorporated into the HMPC state vector, combining a physics-based photovoltaic model with a neural network component to continuously refine the power–voltage curve. The HMPC layer then resolves a receding-horizon optimization problem to ensure reliable global MPPT convergence while limiting control oscillations. To support real-time applicability, a multi-rate sampling strategy and edge-computing implementation are adopted. Experimental assessments confirm that the framework markedly enhances MPPT accuracy across diverse and unpredictable shading environments. The approach is especially valuable for PV installations in densely built urban areas, where dynamic partial shading is frequent and difficult to model accurately.

title: AI-Powered Smart Industrial Automation: Integrating IIoT and Cognitive Systems for Predictive Process Optimization
authors: M. Ravichandran, Raja Govindan, A. Rajalingam, Thaer Ahmad Abu-Saleem, Manish Gupta, G. Karthikeyan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Futuristic Technology
abstract: The use of AI in partnership with the IIoT is changing the face of industrial automation, as we transition from reactive control to predictive and self-optimizing operations. This study investigates the integrated framework of AI-based intelligent industrial automation by integrating with IIoT architectures together with cognitive computing systems. The goal is to drive process efficiency by minimizing downtime and facilitating decision-making in real time in complicated industrial settings. Cognitive systems based on machine learning (ML), deep learning (DL) and natural language processing (NLP) are used to analyze complex and heterogeneous data from IIoT sensors and actuators, spread across manufacturing sites, supply chains and energy systems. Central to such systems is their capability to perform online pattern recognition, anomaly detection as well as predictive maintenance, adaptive control, and context-aware optimization. The model used in the presented work combines unsupervised, supervised, and reinforcement leaning methodologies of AI and exploits them on both sides of time flow into a how time sensors data are processed and into identifying optimal control parameters in a predictive way. We experimentally validate in a smart manufacturing testbed with edge computing infrastructure and cloud-based analytics platforms to demonstrate scalability, latency reduction, and secure data orchestration. The work shows a remarkable enhancement in operation efficiency, energy consumption and fault prediction performance. In addition, it tackles important issues of interoperability, data protection, and man-machine cooperation in cognitive automation ecosystems. By enabling intelligent collaboration between CPSs, AI agents, and ICSs, this work provides the framework for the next generation of autonomous, resilient, sustainable industrial operations.

title: Efficient Fine-Tuning Strategies for Domain-Adaptive Foundation Models in Large-Scale Data Science Applications
authors: Gousia Thahniyath, Benaka Santhosha S, Tanvir Habib Sardar, Sridhar S K, Kumar Dilip, Naitik S T
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Inventive Computation Technologies (ICICT)
abstract: The transfer of large-scale foundation language models to small-scale applications is a severe bottleneck in data science applications that can scale, in particular bottlenecked by computational resources. A hybrid Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) architecture, integrating Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), curriculum learning schedules, and domain specific continual pretraining, is presented in this work, which allows the specialization of foundation models of 7-13 billion parameters effectively. It introduces a new dynamic rank selection algorithm, which adjusts the low-rank matrices on which training is based on gradient statistics and, therefore, does not involve exhaustive hyperparameter search. Evaluations on four domain-specific benchmarks show that the proposed method reduces computational cost by 85.3% compared to full fine-tuning.. It has also been determined that the framework can be generalized and scaled by looking at large ablation studies within the field of biomedical, financial, legal and software engineering. The practical implications of these findings on the practical implementation of foundation models to resource constrained enterprise systems are extensive.

title: Post-Implementation Evaluation of an Integrated Student Information and Faculty Evaluation System using the DeLone and McLean IS Success Model
authors: Jade G. Cuartero
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Inventive Computation Technologies (ICICT)
abstract: This study evaluates the post-implementation success of an integrated Student Information System (SIS) and Faculty Evaluation System (FES) at Agusan del Sur State College of Agriculture and Technology (ASSCAT) using the DeLone and McLean IS Success Model. Digital transformation is critical for higher education efficiency; however, empirical evidence in resource-constrained Philippine State Colleges remains limited. Utilizing a descriptive-evaluative design and a systematic stratified random sample (e=0.05), the study assessed 20 indicators across four dimensions. Results indicate "Very High" scores for Information Quality (M=4.24) and User Satisfaction (M=4.31), particularly regarding real-time grade viewing and financial transparency. System Quality (M=4.12) and Net Benefits (M=4.18) also performed well, though 82.6% of respondents reported connectivity challenges during peak enrollment. These findings confirm that while the integrated system significantly reduces administrative workload and enhances transparency, infrastructure scalability remains a vital requirement for long-term sustainability. This research provides a methodical framework for HEIs to link user perception with organizational outcomes in the digital era.

title: Enhancing Industrial Ethernet Performance: A Comparative Study of PROFINET Software Development Kit with AF_PACKET and XDP
authors: Lokman Altın, Hayri Mutlu, Ulaş Akın, Ali Argun Berberoğlu, Ömer Korçak
publish: 2026 IEEE 22nd International Conference on Factory Communication Systems (WFCS)
abstract: Fast and predictable packet processing is essential for time-sensitive PROFINET communication on Linux-based controllers. In this context, the Siemens PROFINET Software Development Kit (PN SDK) provides a widely used toolkit for implementing PROFINET controllers and devices. In current Linux-based deployments, the PN SDK commonly relies on the AF_PACKET path to capture, process and steer Ethernet frames; however, stack-level overhead can limit throughput consistency and robustness under heavy load. Recent Linux kernel technologies based on eBPF and eXpress Data Path (XDP) enable earlier packet handling and efficient packet steering, offering a promising alternative for industrial real-time communication stacks. This paper integrates an XDP-based processing path into the PN SDK controller runtime and provides a comparative evaluation against the AF_PACKET-based variant using (i) iperf3 throughput/jitter measurements and (ii) certificationoriented PROFINET Netload robustness tests. The results show that the XDP-based variant removes the need for stack-level NRT rate limiting in the tested setup and substantially improves timing stability while maintaining PROFINET communication reliability under stress. Across Netload Faulty Communication tests, the XDP variant yields on average approximately 61% fewer AR drops and 49% lower total downtime than AF_PACKET, indicating that early in-driver processing via XDP can improve throughput consistency and robustness for production-grade PROFINET RT controllers.

title: A Comprehensive Review of English-Tamil Machine Translation from Statistical Models to Neuro-Symbolic Approaches
authors: Matan P, P Velvizhy, Krishna Priya K, Steffy Sherly P
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Inventive Computation Technologies (ICICT)
abstract: Machine Translation (MT) has witnessed remarkable advancements, transitioning from rule-based systems to statistical models and, more recently, to data-driven neural approaches. While Neural Machine Translation (NMT) has become the state-of-the-art, its effectiveness largely depends on the availability of large parallel corpora, posing significant limitations for low-resource language pairs such as English-Tamil. This paper presents a comprehensive review of the evolution of MT techniques, focusing particularly on the challenges and developments in English-Tamil translation. This paper explores the linguistic complexities, data scarcity issues, and domain-specific constraints that hinder current NMT systems in producing contextually and grammatically accurate Tamil translations. The study highlights recent efforts in hybrid and transfer learning models and introduces a promising direction-Neuro-Symbolic Machine Translation (NSMT). By integrating symbolic linguistic knowledge, such as Universal Networking Language (UNL), with neural models, NSMT aims to leverage both structured grammatical reasoning and powerful sequence modeling. This approach is particularly suited for morphologically rich and syntactically distant languages. The paper reviews the research gaps and limitations by outlining the design of a proposing a NSMT framework for English-Tamil translation and discussing future research avenues to enhance translation quality, interpretability, and language preservation in the Indian linguistic context.

title: Beyond Data: A Privacy-Preserving IoT Framework for Personalized and Behavior-Based Health Interventions
authors: Mohd Adnan, Pooja Devi, Rakesh Kumar, Nagendar Yamsani, Harsh Awashti
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Futuristic Technology
abstract: The widespread adoption of wearable Internet of Things (IoT) devices has generated a wealth of health data, yet the effective translation of this data into personalized, actionable health interventions remains a significant challenge due to data fragmentation, a lack of personalization, and critical privacy concerns. This paper proposes a novel, three-layered system architecture, the Adaptive and Privacy-Preserving Health Intervention System (APPHIS), designed to overcome these limitations. Our model integrates multi-modal data from various wearable sensors and employs an on-device machine learning model to identify health patterns while keeping sensitive raw data local to the user. The system's behavioral intervention engine, grounded in established models, delivers context-aware, personalized recommendations to enhance user engagement and promote sustained behavioral change. Through this approach, APPHIS provides a robust and ethical framework for personalized health, demonstrating that highly effective solutions can be achieved without compromising user data security.

title: Real-time Handwritten Digit Recognition: CNN Model, Digits Recognition and Streamlit Deployment
authors: R. Sathesh Raaj, Sanjai.B, Ganesh.Y, Subiksha.A.G, Bikash Ranjan Behera
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Futuristic Technology
abstract: The need for dependable and accurate handwritten digit recognition systems has grown in academic financial,and document processing settings due to the increasing trend toward automated digital processes. This work presents a real-time handwritten digit recognition model constructed using a lightweight Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), utilizing the robust feature-learning capabilities of contemporary deeplearning techniques. Effective learning of spatial variations and writing patterns is made possible by the model’s training on the MNIST dataset, which contains 70,000 grayscale handwritten digit samples. A number of traditional machine-learning models,such as XGBoost, Random Forest, K-Nearest Neighbors, and Logistic Regression, were also used to benchmark performance.According to experimental analysis, the suggested CNN exhibits strong generalization, achieving 99% training accuracy and 98% validation accuracy.To increase robustness for both single-digit and multi-digit recognition, the system incorporates preprocessing techniques like contour-based digit segmentation, adaptive thresholding, and grayscale conversion. The system is appropriate for real-world use cases like form digitization, automated entry,and education-focused applications because a Streamlit-based web interface was created to enable real-time digit drawing and digit upload.

title: Explainable Hybrid CNN Framework for Accurate Multi-Crop Disease Detection and Yield Enhancement
authors: Swapnil P. Bangal, Satish R. Jondhale, Sachin V. Chaudhari, Balasaheb S. Agarkar
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Inventive Computation Technologies (ICICT)
abstract: Global agriculture is seriously threatened by plant diseases, which drastically reduce crop yields, economic productivity, and food security. Conventional methods of disease detection rely on manual inspections, which are time-consuming, error-prone, and often inconsistent under various field conditions. To overcome these limitations, the current study proposes an explainable hybrid CNN (Convolutional neural networks) method for precise plant disease identification and yield enhancement. The proposed architecture combines CNNs for dependable feature extraction with classifiers that make complex decisions using machine learning. A comprehensive preprocessing pipeline that incorporates noise reduction, augmentation, and normalization improves the model's predictive power in a variety of lighting scenarios, crop types, and occlusions. Explainable AI (XAI) methods, like Grad-CAM, demonstrate the important elements affecting classification results, improving interpretability and boosting user confidence. Even in the face of challenging lighting and background changes, experimental evaluations show superior efficacy over traditional models, achieving high F1-scores, recall, accuracy, and precision. Furthermore, by including yield-related insights that support data-driven agricultural decision-making, the methodology extends beyond simply identifying illnesses. A comprehensive strategy reduces crop losses, promotes sustainable farming methods, and allows for proactive disease management. For modern precision agriculture, the suggested system provides an open, scalable, and useful solution.

title: Enhancing Cloud Security: A Hybrid Approach to RAT Detection using Shannon Entropy and Network Jitter Analysis
authors: Gopika J, Andrea S, Sindhu T
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Futuristic Technology
abstract: The rapid move to cloud platforms has increased the threat of advanced, evasive Remote Access Trojans that bypass traditional signature-based defenses. This research proposes a Cloud-Native Hybrid Extended Detection and Response framework that offers detection of zero-day threats by means of behavioral anomaly correlation. Such methodology leverages a two-layered analysis architecture: Shannon Entropy to identify high randomness memory segments, which would indicate packed malware, and analyzing Network Jitter-Inter-Arrival Time variance-to differentiate robotic command-and-control beaconing from valid human traffic. By synthesizing host and network signals into a single Threat Confidence Score, the system significantly reduces false positives and ascertains that alerts come backed by evidence in several domains. In addition, this framework integrates automated mitigations through cloud APIs for the immediate isolation of instances that are compromised, making it a highly scalable solution without dependence on any particular vendor for hardening the contemporary cloud infrastructure against constant threats.

title: Quality Weighted Multipoint Depth Buffer Visibility and Coverage Planning for Robotic 3D Structural Inspection
authors: Ashish Deelip Ghatge, Matthew Turnowicz, Pranav Nedunghat
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Robotics and Technologies for Industrial Automation (ROBOTHIA)
abstract: Robotic inspection of structures requires complete visual coverage of the target surface. This is critical, especially for 3D reconstruction applications. Most existing offline coverage path planners treat geometric visibility as a sufficient proxy for inspection data quality. Typical coverage path planners for robotic structural inspection evaluate visibility of a mesh element as a binary predicate (either seen or not). But geometric visibility does not automatically guarantee good data quality. This paper demonstrates that binary coverage systematically overestimates inspection quality. We identified following three sources of overestimation. Grazing incidence angles that reduce effective pixel density, large mesh triangles on flat surfaces that exceed a single camera footprint, and the absence of any mechanism to verify planned coverage against actual reconstruction data. To address these issues, we propose a quality weighted coverage metric based on the angle of incidence between the camera viewing direction and the surface normal of the mesh triangle. The metric is computed along with a multipoint depth buffer visibility test in a single GPU accelerated pass. This provides real time coverage feedback as operators create inspection waypoints. A verification architecture using registered dense point clouds detects coverage gaps and model to reality discrepancies during execution. Experiments were done on five different structural meshes (150 K to 6.2 M triangles) to measure the gap between binary and quality weighted coverage. It was found that binary assessment overestimates actual viewing quality by 2.7 to 7.5% (5.5% average) across meshes of varying size and complexity.

title: Multifactor Student Academic Performance Classification using Machine Learning
authors: Epifelward Niño O. Amora, Marc Benigno Olaguir
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Inventive Computation Technologies (ICICT)
abstract: Academic performance prediction is a key application of educational data mining, enabling early identification of at-risk students and data-driven intervention strategies. This study presents a multifactor machine learning framework for classifying student academic performance using a dataset of 6,607 secondary school students. The dataset integrates academic indicators, study behaviors, parental involvement, lifestyle variables, and socio-demographic attributes. Exam scores were discretized into four categories— Low, Average, High, and Excellent—forming a multi-class classification task. Five supervised algorithms were evaluated: Logistic Regression, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, Support Vector Machine, and k-Nearest Neighbors. Preprocessing included imputation, categorical encoding, feature standardization, and stratified train-test splitting. Model performance was assessed using Accuracy, Balanced Accuracy, weighted Precision/Recall/F1, and Matthews Correlation Coefficient. Logistic Regression achieved the best performance, outperforming more complex models while maintaining interpretability. Key predictors include prior academic performance, study time, attendance, and parental involvement. The framework supports early-warning systems and targeted academic interventions.

title: BERT - Based Evaluation of Depression Detection Systems on Social Media
authors: Lakpathwar Varshini, Shaik Jakeer Hussain, Vyshnavi Pola, Bandari Vignesh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Futuristic Technology
abstract: This project aims to detect depression from social media text, meeting the increasing worldwide need for dealing with mental health issues and early diagnosis problems. Depression, which is marked by persistent sadness, hopelessness, and diminished interest in daily activities, usually goes unnoticed because of stigma, limited awareness, and inaccessible mental health care. With the advent of social media sites, users often post psychological distress on these platforms, presenting a chance for early detection. To combat the difficulty of handling enormous amounts of user data, different machine learning as well as deep learning architectures have been used to categorize depression posts. Among the models evaluated, the Transformer-based BERT model achieved a high accuracy of 96%, second by BiLSTM at 94%, and CNN-BiLSTM and LSTM-GRU models, each with 93%. The LSTM model reached 85% accuracy, while the conventional SVM-GRU combination showed the lowest performance at 75%. These results highlight the superiority of Transformer-based architectures over traditional and hybrid deep learning models in this context. Performance measures such as classification Accuracy, Precision, Recall and F1-Score, Support were measured to analyze the efficacy of the used models. Findings validate a superiority of deep learning-based methods, particularly transformer models, in the effective identification of indicators of depression in social media posts, thereby validating their potential for early intervention and assistance.

title: ViT-Graph: Ransomware Detection via Vision Transformer Classification of Yifan Hu-Visualized Control Flow Graphs
authors: Muhammad Aimaan, Ahmad Firdaus, Muhammad Syafiq, Nur Khairani Kamarudin, Mohd Faizal Ab Razak
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Robotics and Technologies for Industrial Automation (ROBOTHIA)
abstract: The increasing complexity of ransomware demands a departure from heuristic methods based on static signatures, which are likely to fail when dealing with obfuscated or packed binaries. Existing techniques such as raw binary visualization, capture file statistics but ignore the critical features. In this paper, we propose a new structural detection pipeline to address this gap by transforming the program's inherent behavior into an image-like topology for classification with ViTs. Our approach consists of three stages: (1) the extraction of the Inter-procedural Control Flow Graph (ICFG) from a Portable Executable (PE) file; (2) the graphical representation ICFG using Yifan Hu's Multi-Level force directed layout in order to highlight structural patterns and (3) labeling with our fine-tuned model google/vit-base-patch16-224 on the resulting image. Experimented on a pilot dataset of 2529 Benign and Ransomware samples, the resulting framework attained an Accuracy of 97% that is notably higher than the original Convolutional Neural Network (ResNet-50: 84%, VGG-16: 93%) and Swin Transformers (95%) and graph-based baselines (CGN:50%, ViT-GNN: 93,7). This work shows the effectiveness of translating program logic into the visual domain for robust structure-based ransomware detection, offering a promising direction to combat advanced threats.

title: Optimizing Cross-Domain Assurance: A Signaling Approach to Reduce Accreditation Costs
authors: Imran Ali Mohammed
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Futuristic Technology
abstract: This paper addresses a critical challenge in Information Assurance: the significant inefficiencies and redundant efforts inherent in the accreditation of classified cross-domain systems. We identify information asymmetry among multiple security accreditors as a primary driver of these complexities, leading to duplicated security testing and unnecessary risk mitigations. By applying the principles of economic signaling theory to the domain of information assurance, this research proposes a novel conceptual framework for formalizing communication signals. This approach aims to establish a more transparent and efficient route to consensus on residual risk levels, thereby streamlining the accreditation process. The theoretical insights presented herein demonstrate the potential to substantially reduce the high financial and operational burdens associated with assuring complex, multi-domain information systems, paving the way for more agile and cost-effective security governance.

title: Fast Cylindrical Explicit and Unconditionally Stable FDTD Method Involving Fine Structure
authors: Sihan Zhao, Xinbo He, Bing Wei
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: A fast cylindrical explicit and unconditionally stable finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) method that accommodates fine structures is proposed. This method introduces a general domain decomposition strategy derived from an analysis of the spectral radius of cylindrical grids. This strategy applies to a wide range of grid discretizations, especially in cases involving fine grids. By doing so, all unstable modes can be obtained by solving the eigenvalue problem in one subdomain, significantly reducing the computational complexity. Two explicit field-iteration schemes are also presented. In addition, the boundary fields between subdomains require no additional correction. Numerical results demonstrate the validity and efficiency of the proposed method.

title: OBES-Net: An Exhaustively Optimized Explainable Ensemble Framework for Robust Obesity Risk Prediction
authors: Towhidul Islam, Md Sumon Ali, Md Moazzem Hossain, Md Shafiullah, Md Shafiul Alam
abstract: Obesity is a global health crisis requiring accurate and interpretable prediction models for clinical use. Current ensemble approaches lack systematic comparisons and often use suboptimal configurations. This study introduces OBES-Net, a novel framework that integrates three methodological advances: (1) exhaustive hyperparameter optimization across 50 configurations of nine machine learning algorithms, (2) systematic comparison of Hybrid Majority Voting strategies (Majority Hard vs. Weighted Hard) against Ensemble Stacking, and (3) comprehensive Explainable AI (XAI) validation using SHAP and LIME. Utilizing two diverse datasets, OBES-Net demonstrates that Stacking achieves superior performance on complex data (Accuracy: 0.991 on Dataset-2), while voting strategies remain competitive on simpler tasks. Crucially, the proposed XAI analyses reveal that Stacking’s advantage stems from synthesizing broader clinical risk factors, withWeight and Age emerging as consistent primary drivers. Extensive ablation studies validate the robustness of the proposed design choices. The OBES-Net framework establishes that combining exhaustive optimization with systematic ensemble comparison and XAI delivers both state-of-the-art performance and clinical trustworthiness, providing an advancing methodology for healthcare decision-making.

title: SBF: A Spectral Bands Fusion Framework for Hyperspectral Scene Classification
authors: Chengyi Yang, Xiangtao Zheng, Tian Gao, Xiaoqiang Lu
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Hyperspectral scene classification aims to identify the scene categories for hyperspectral images, which is difficult to extract the joint spatial-spectral features. This letter proposes a Spectral Bands Fusion (SBF) framework with two stages: grouped band fusion and ensemble classification. In first stage, hyperspectral image is decomposed into different band groups, and then a dominant image is selected to fuse with each group to acquire enhanced band groups. In second stage, multiple base classification models are fine-tuned on these enhanced band groups, where a dynamic ensemble strategy is proposed to adaptively integrate the output of each base model. Experimental results show SBF achieves state-of-the-art performance. The code is available at https://github.com/XiangtaoZheng/SBF.

title: Generating Test Cases for Autonomous Vehicles with Controllable Levels of Difficulty
authors: Ziyu Wang, Jing Ma, Edmund M-K Lai
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Autonomous vehicle (AV) safety validation increasingly relies on scenario-based testing. However, existing approaches to test scenario generation do not provide mechanisms to systematically regulate scenario difficulty. To address this critical limitation, this paper introduces a novel game-theoretic framework for adversarial safety validation. The interaction between the AV-under-test and a strategically obstructing rear vehicle is modelled as a Stackelberg game. The level of adversarial intensity, which reflects the level of difficulty, of a scenario can be controlled by a single tunable parameter called aggressiveness at both the action level and the interaction level. The efficacy of this approach is studied through the highway lane-changing operational design domain. Simulation experiments demonstrate that increasing aggressiveness reduces the success rate of lane-changing and prolongs maneuver duration for the successful attempts. These results confirm that this parameter can effectively and systematically control the difficulty level of test scenarios, providing a valuable tool for rigorous and reproducible AV safety validation.

title: Dense Retrieval for Electronic Health Record With Knowledge Injection and Synthetic Data
authors: Zhengyun Zhao, Huaiyuan Ying, Sheng Yu
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Electronic Health Records (EHRs) are pivotal in clinical practices, yet their retrieval remains a challenge mainly due to semantic gap issues. Recent advancements in dense retrieval offer promising solutions but existing models, both general-domain and biomedical-domain, fall short due to insufficient medical knowledge or mismatched training corpora. Previous EHR retrievers generally focus on a small set of queries and fail to generalize. This paper introduces DR.EHR, a series of dense retrieval models specifically tailored for EHR retrieval. We propose a two-stage training pipeline utilizing MIMIC-IV discharge summaries to address the need for extensive medical knowledge and large-scale training data. The first stage involves medical entity extraction and knowledge injection from a biomedical knowledge graph, while the second stage employs large language models to generate diverse training data. We train two variants of DR.EHR, with 110 M and 7B parameters, respectively. Evaluated on the CliniQ benchmark, our models significantly outperforms all existing dense retrievers, achieving state-of-the-art results. Detailed analyses confirm our models' superiority across various match and query types, particularly in challenging semantic matches such as implication and abbreviation. Ablation studies validate the effectiveness of each pipeline component, and supplementary experiments on EHR QA datasets demonstrate the models' generalizability on various EHR corpus and natural language questions including complex ones with multiple entities. This work significantly advances EHR retrieval, offering a robust solution for clinical applications.

title: Field evaluation and optimization of a lightweight autonomous lidar-based UAV system based on a rigorous experimental setup in boreal forest environments_supp1-3691711.mp4
