title: SWD-Net: A Benchmark Dataset for Small Water Body Detection from Very High-Resolution Imagery
authors: Di Liu, Chengbin Deng, Pontus Olofsson, Songkun Yan, Jiao Wang, Isabelle Hu, Yang Hong
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Small and seasonal water bodies store a substantial portion of inland surface water and play a key role in local hydrology, ecosystem resilience, and agricultural sustainability. Yet their small size, irregular geometry, and frequent seasonal variability hinder accurate detection in global water products derived from coarse-resolution imagery. To address this limitation, we present Small Water Body Detection (SWD-Net), a high-resolution, expert-annotated benchmark dataset for small water-body detection from aerial and satellite imagery. SWD-Net consists of 10,085 image–mask pairs (1,024 × 1,024 pixels) sourced primarily from NAIP, Google Earth, and LoveDA, spanning diverse climatic and land-cover conditions. Baseline experiments using five representative segmentation architectures, including U-Net, Mamba U-Net, ViT U-Net, SegFormer U-Net, and Swin U-Net, demonstrate clear architectural contrasts, with transformer-based networks achieving the most accurate delineation of fine-scale and fragmented ponds. Among them, Swin U-Net achieved the best overall performance, with an IoU of 93.53% and an F1-score of 96.56% on the test set. All data, pretrained weights, and evaluation codes are publicly released to facilitate transparent and reproducible research in fine-scale hydrological mapping, and seasonal water monitoring.

title: Risk-Sensitive Control With Multi-Armed Bandit Adaptive Sampling
authors: Brian W. Rybicki, Jill K. Nelson
abstract: We propose a risk-sensitive (RS) control algorithm that optimizes the entropic risk measure (ERM). RS control minimizes the impact of high cost but low probability outcomes in dynamic decision making. ERM is a time-consistent, convex measure of risk with tunable sensitivity. Our ERM-sensitive method combines online adaptive multistage sampling, RS upper confidence bound action selection, and sample-based estimation of ERM. Unlike many other RS control approaches, our method is applicable to problems with both discrete and continuous state spaces, and it does not require knowledge of the underlying transition probabilities. This makes our approach particularly useful in a wide variety of RS reinforcement learning domains. We show <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$O(\log M / M)$</tex-math></inline-formula> errorbounded convergence under mild conditions and demonstrate the numerical performance on two RS benchmark problems with discrete and continuous state spaces, respectively.

title: Asynchronous Space-Time-Coding Digital Metasurface with Full-Phase Tunability for Arbitrary Multiple Target Generation
authors: Si Ran Wang, Shao Nan Chen, Zhen Jie Qi, Xin Yu Fang, Qun Yan Zhou, Jun Wei Zhang, Jun Yan Dai, Qiang Cheng, Geng-Bo Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: Conventional active radar jamming relies on digital radio frequency memory techniques to manipulate radar signals but suffers from bulky radio-frequency (RF) chains, high power consumption, and complex hardware. Space-time-coding metasurfaces (STCMs), with subwavelength-scale phase control and RF-level signal processing capabilities, offer a promising passive alternative. However, existing STCM-based jamming schemes exhibit range-Doppler periodicity among multiple generated targets for FMCW radars, leading to potential jamming failure. This work clarifies the origin of this periodicity and proposes a full-phase asynchronous STCM (ASTCM) architecture to overcome it. Specifically, the ASTCM is divided into different modulated frequency partitions. Leveraging full-phase tunability, each partition generates a pure spectrally isolated false target. From both the system-level and engineering perspectives, this architecture breaks the range-Doppler periodicity among multiple targets, allowing independent and flexible control over their ranges and velocities. Experimental results confirm the feasibility of multi-target generation, demonstrating a low-cost, programmable platform for electronic countermeasures, radar system testing, and data collection for learning-based applications.

title: TGS-SLAM: Tri-plane Gaussian Splatting for Semantic SLAM
authors: Guoxi Sun, Handong Shen, Xiaohao Liu, Xinchao Li, Lingyu Liang, Beibei Liu, Shuangping Huang
abstract: Semantic SLAM aims to build 3D maps that are both geometrically accurate and semantically consistent for downstream tasks such as navigation, manipulation, and AR/VR. Existing dense neural approaches largely follow two paradigms: implicit neural fields, which offer strong cross-view consistency but rely on expensive NeRF-style volumetric rendering, and 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS), which is efficient but typically attaches semantics to individual Gaussians, yielding fragmented primitive-level semantics and semantic parameters that scale linearly with the number of primitives. We present TGS-SLAM, a semantic SLAM system that anchors geometry and appearance with 3D Gaussians while encoding semantics in TriDS, a shared coarse-to-fine tri-plane field decoupled from Gaussian primitives. Semantic features are queried at Gaussian centers, decoded into class logits, and splatted jointly with color and depth in a single rendering pass. We further adopt a geometry-first optimization schedule to stabilize the Gaussian map before semantic learning, and a hybrid pose initialization mechanism for robust tracking. Experiments on Replica, ScanNet, and ScanNet++ demonstrate that TGS-SLAM produces coherent semantic maps with competitive tracking and reconstruction quality. On Replica, it achieves 97.0% mIoU while reducing semantic parameters by $\sim 100\times$ compared with per-Gaussian one-hot encodings. Code is available at https://github.com/shand001/TGS-SLAM.

title: Multilevel Feature Fusion and Nonparametric Detection of Furnace Temperature States in Municipal Solid Waste Incineration Systems_supp1-3687100.docx
authors: Heng Xia
abstract: The furnace temperature is a critical operational indicator for ensuring the safe and stable operation of municipal solid waste incineration (MSWI) processes. However, temperature data often exhibit strong nonlinear, multidimensional, and coupling characteristics, which pose significant challenges for the qualitative analysis of state changes. To address these issues, this study proposes a novel qualitative temperature state analysis method based on multilevel feature fusion and nonparametric detection. First, feature analysis is integrated into tree-based models to distinguish strongly and weakly relevant features, after which statistical fusion and a variational autoencoder are used to characterize the two types. Next, the fusion feature space is further optimized by incorporating information contribution, resulting in a compact yet informative feature representation. Finally, a nonparametric two-sided detection method is applied to identify changepoints and construct qualitative state datasets. In practical deployments, the availability of ground-truth state labels would enable more rigorous quantitative comparison and validation; however, such labels are often unavailable in real-world MSWI systems. In such cases, the proposed method offers an auxiliary reference for evaluation in unlabeled scenarios. Experiments conducted on real MSWI datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method in accurately capturing temperature state transitions and generating high-quality state data.

title: Towards Robust Train-Approach Detection with UAV-Assisted Networks: Survey and A Multiscale Texture-Aware Mechanism
authors: Ruiheng Yang, Mingyuan Liu, Yuming Zhang, Ruibing Guo, Haokun Li, Qixuan Zhu, Yida Gu, Wei Quan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: Accurate detection of approaching trains is essential for railway maintenance safety. However, it remains challenging in complex maintenance scenarios with diverse illumination conditions. Existing methods struggle to capture multiscale train features and texture cues, leading to frequent missed detections. This paper proposes a three-stage Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV)-assisted Enhanced Multiscale Texture-aware (UEMT) mechanism for train-approach detection. First, an adaptive modality selection strategy is designed to select the optimal modality from UAV-captured visible-light (RGB) and infrared (IR) video frames, to maintain stable input under varying illumination conditions. Second, an enhanced detection network integrating deformable convolution and train-oriented attention modules is proposed to improve multiscale representation and texture discrimination. Third, an aggressive gap-filling mechanism is introduced to reduce missed detections and ensure reliable detection across video frames. We construct a UAV-based train dataset for various high-speed railway scenes, which contains over 10,000 RGB samples and over 2,500 paired bimodal RGB-IR samples under diverse weather conditions, backgrounds, and viewpoints. Experimental results show that the proposed mechanism improves mAP50 by 10.47%, 6.72%, and 7.12% over baseline methods under low-, medium-, and high-illumination conditions, respectively, while maintaining low computational cost and extremely low miss and false alarm rates.

title: Active Damping Strategy for Inverter-Side Inductor Voltage Integral Feedback to Suppress DC Offset
authors: Di WU, Weiyu YU, Mingming LI
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Power Electronics
abstract: Integrating the voltage across the inverter-side inductor for feedback effectively suppresses resonance spikes in LCL-type filters. Due to the low-pass filtering characteristic of integration, it also effectively suppresses high-frequency switching noise, improving the smoothness of the feedback signal. Compared to conventional feedback filter capacitor current measurement, this approach significantly enhances the system's output impedance. However, traditional pure integrators are highly sensitive to DC bias in the input signal, easily leading to integrator saturation. This can compromise resonance suppression effectiveness and even threaten system stability. To address this, this paper proposes an active damping strategy for inverter-side inductor voltage feedback based on a third-order generalized integrator. By incorporating a DC suppression loop into the second-order generalized integrator (SOGI), this strategy achieves ideal integration characteristics at fundamental frequencies while effectively attenuating DC bias and higher harmonics in the input signal. Theoretical analysis and experimental results demonstrate the strategy's superior performance in suppressing resonance and overcoming integration saturation. It significantly enhances system robustness and grid-connected current quality, thereby achieving highly robust active damping control.

title: Non-Uniform Metasurface-Based Millimeter-Wave Antenna with Broadband Circular Polarization and Stable Gain
authors: Shu-An Lin, Eric S. Li, Chieh Hua Wu, Jia Yan Yang, Kuo-Sheng Chin, Cristiano Tomassoni
abstract: This work presents a millimeter-wave (mmWave) broadband circularly polarized (CP) antenna featuring a non-uniform metasurface (MS) and stable gain for 5G applications. A comprehensive analysis of the CP mechanism in corner-truncated patch MS is conducted. The design is planned out to begin with a uniform 4×4 MS (Antenna I), yielding a simulated axial-ratio (AR) bandwidth of 12.9%. By optimizing the size and spacing of non-uniform cells (Antenna II), the bandwidth expands to 21.6%. A novel 2D phase-difference distribution method is developed to elucidate regional contributions, facilitating targeted geometry optimization. Consequently, central and peripheral regions govern low- and high-frequency resonant modes, respectively, thereby broadening the AR bandwidth. To suppress sidelobe levels (SLL), transverse diagonal slits are introduced in Antenna III to attenuate currents in peripheral cells, concentrating current intensity in central cells. The impact of substrate enlargement to accommodate a GCPW-to-SIW transition is investigated. A coplanar grounding structure is proposed to mitigate surface wave excitation, ensuring SLLs remain below −10.8 dB. The simulated 10-dB impedance bandwidth, AR bandwidth, and realized gain of the finalized antenna are 24.17–36.86 GHz (41.6%), 27.87–34.19 GHz (20.4%), and 9 dBic, respectively, whereas the measured counterparts are 24.9–33.5 GHz (29.5%), 28.9–33.9 GHz (16%), and 8.1 dBic. In addition, the measured 2-dB gain bandwidth is 19.7%. These features support the proposed antenna as a robust solution for 5G mmWave systems.

title: A Novel Kinematically Redundant Orthopedic Robot for Avoiding Interference in Long Bone Deformity Correction
authors: Jianfeng Li, Qianhui Ma, Jie Ju, Tuxian Ye, Mingjie Dong, Shiping Zuo
abstract: Long bone deformity correction with orthopedic robots is guided by a preplanned correction path, and the continuous implementation of the entire process is highly important for achieving acceptable outcomes. However, multiple types of interference phenomena (i.e., strut overstroke, singularity configuration, and soft tissue-strut collision) frequently occur, which necessitate temporary robot reassembly and path replanning that interrupt the correction process. To solve this issue, this letter introduces the self-motion characteristics of redundant parallel mechanisms into the design of orthopedic robots. On this basis, a novel redundant configuration is proposed, and its mechatronic design is developed. Afterward, on the basis of the kinematic model and the cylinder-elliptical table analytical model, avoidance indices are presented for predictiong the interference state. By integrating the indices and performing weight discrimination, a configuration optimization algorithm is constructed for all interference avoidance tasks. Finally, simulations and prototype experiments demonstrate that the proposed design concept and methodology can assist surgeons in planning an interference-free adjustment scheme and ensure the continuity of the correction process without altering the original robot assembly and correction path.

title: ASA-ED: Automated Stress Assessment Via Emotion-Awareness-Driven Deep Hybrid Learning Fusing MTF and RP
authors: Mi Li, Yanbo Chen, Junzhe Li, Shengfu Lu, Bin Hu
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Currently, most studies on mental stress evaluation mainly focus on classification tasks, while research on accurately estimating continuous stress levels using deep learning for early identification remains limited. This study proposes an end-to-end continuous stress assessment framework based on a deep hybrid learning architecture. The framework employs efficient channel attention convolution to extract local pattern features from the signals, utilizes a bidirectional long short-term memory (BiLSTM) network to model contextual dependencies, and incorporates emotional cross-attention to assign importance weights to different emotional states. In addition, an adaptive ridge stacking ensemble learning method is proposed. To enhance feature representation, pulse rate variability (PRV) and discrete pulse signals (dPS) extracted from Photoplethysmography (PPG) signals are encoded into markov transition field (MTF) and recurrence plot (RP) images, respectively. The results show that, for PRV, MTF- and RP-based representations reduce the detection error by 6.93% and 2.57% compared with the time-domain baseline. For dPS, the error reductions reach 6.97% and 15.05%. Furthermore, the proposed fusion strategy of PRV-MTF and dPS-RP achieves the best performance (MAE = 3.29, RMSE = 4.05). Compared with the previous state-of-the-art method based on time-domain fusion of PRV and dPS signals (MAE = 4.38, RMSE = 5.19), the proposed approach yields substantial reductions of 24.88% in MAE and 21.96% in RMSE, reaching the current state-of-the-art performance. These results demonstrate that transforming time-domain signals into structured encoding images enables more effective capture of deep patterns associated with psychological states, thereby significantly improving the accuracy of mental health detection.

title: Estimating Human Finger Poses via Tactile Mechanics Modeling_supp1-3687294.pdf
authors: Jixiao Liu
abstract: Understanding human intentions based on tactile sensing is pivotal for enabling seamless and intuitive human-robot interaction (HRI). By interpreting physical contact, robots can infer human goals, adjust their behavior in real time, and enhance collaboration in shared environments. However, robots cannot fully understand human intent solely from tactile information and often need to rely on other modalities, such as vision. The tactile information generated by finger contact is intrinsically linked to the corresponding motion. Hence, this paper presents a method that estimates human finger motion directly from tactile features obtained during fingertip contact. Motion parameters are represented by finger poses, and tactile features are extracted as contact area, contact position, etc. A linear model is first proposed to describe the relationship between the contact angle and the contact area. To account for the effect of contact forces on the linear relationship, a three-layer structure model (TLSM) of the finger is introduced to characterize the relationship between contact force and contact area. Combining the two models, the contact angle can be estimated. Then, a local pressure center (LPC) algorithm is proposed to determine the contact position of the fingertip. Finally, inverse kinematics is applied to estimate joint angles and reconstruct finger poses. This work developed a method for estimating finger poses via tactile features, which could enhance the perceptual capabilities of robotic systems and be suitable for human-robot interaction.

title: Preface
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 IIEEJ International Conference on Image Electronics and Visual Computing (IEVC)
abstract: The 9th IIEEJ International Conference on Image Electronics and Visual Computing (IEVC 2026) was held at Hiroshima University, Hiroshima, Japan, from March 16 to 19, 2026. The conference was organized by the Institute of Image Electronics Engineers of Japan (IIEEJ), bringing together researchers, engineers, and students from academia and industry to exchange ideas and discuss recent advances in image electronics and visual computing.

title: Retrieving Geothermal Heat Flow of the Moon Using Chang’e-2 Microwave Radiometer Data
authors: Zhiguo Meng, Xigang Wang, Shengbo Chen, Yi Xu, Xiaoping Zhang, Jinsong Ping, Qiugang Zong, Jietao Lei, Long Xiao, Weiming Cheng, Yuanzhi Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: The geothermal heat flow (HF) is critical to understanding the interior composition, structure, and thermal evolution of the Moon. Microwave radiometer (MRM) data are susceptible to the temperature and compositions of the substrate, making them suitable for retrieving HF information. However, existing HF retrieval methods are limited by retrieval uncertainty and calibration issues in MRM data. To address these issues, a novel HF retrieval model was constructed with the brightness temperature (TB) simulation model by constraining thermophysical parameters using Apollo in-suit HF experiments and Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter Camera Diviner data, and a normalized TB (nTB) mapping scheme to avoid the calibration issue of the MRM data. Utilizing identified optimal TB data derived from Chang’e-2 MRM observations, the HF on a global scale was retrieved utilizing the location-specific HF retrieval models. The main results are as follows. (1) The HF values range from 0 to 66.7 mW∙m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-2</sup>, with an average of 18.1 mW∙m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-2</sup>. (2) Four HF distribution patterns were proposed, indicating that the distribution of radiogenic heat-producing elements derived from the Lunar Prospector Gamma-Ray Spectrometer data cannot represent the vertical distribution throughout the entire crust. (3) There probably exists the localized enrichment of heat-producing elements below the lunar crust, which shows a strong correlation with the basins with diameter ranging from 244 to 380 km. This study is helpful to improve our understanding of the thermal evolution and interior dynamics of the Moon.

title: Flexible Coupler Array with Reconfigurable Pattern: Mechanical Beamforming and Digital Agent
authors: Xiaodan Shao, Yixiao Zhang, Nan Cheng, Weihua Zhuang, Xuemin Shen
abstract: This paper proposes a novel flexible coupler antenna array that incorporates additional degrees of freedom (DoF) in radiation pattern reconfiguration to achieve strong mechanical beamforming gains and enhanced communication coverage with low hardware cost. Particularly, passive couplers move around a fixed active antenna so that the induced currents on the passive elements can be reshaped to achieve radiation pattern reconfiguration. A new form of mechanical beamforming can be obtained by moving only the passive couplers while keeping the active antenna stationary. In addition, the flexible coupler antenna can slide along a rail toward users, thereby enhancing communication coverage. To fully exploit the potential of the flexible coupler array, we formulate a two-timescale sum-rate maximization problem with statistical channel state information (CSI). The active antenna position is optimized based on scattering cluster-core statistics in the slow timescale, while mechanical beamforming is optimized based on multipath channel statistics in the fast timescale, subject to movement and energy constraints. To address the coupling between timescales and the high cost of extensive channel sampling, we develop a digital agent framework that leverages an electromagnetic (EM) map to generate statistical channel information for different user and antenna positions. Then, a deep neural network is trained to learn a slow-fast performance (SFP) surrogate, which is fine-tuned with a small number of real measurements and then applied for position optimization at the slow timescale using projected gradient ascent. Mechanical beamforming at the fast timescale is obtained by selecting per-antenna radiation patterns from a predefined dictionary via a convex relaxation. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed flexible coupler array significantly improves system throughput, and the digital agent-assisted algorithm achieves satisfactory performance with greatly reduced online computational complexity.

title: Minimum Distance Constraint FIR Filter for Dual-foot-mounted Pedestrian Localization
authors: Yuan Xu, Yuriy S. Shmaliy, Miguel A. Vázquez-Olguýn, Xiyuan Chen, Yuan Zhuang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: One of the widely used approaches in the design of self-constraint navigation systems is the zero velocity update (ZUPT) method using a foot-mounted inertial measurement unit (IMU). In this work, we focus on the data filtering using an unbiased finite impulse response (UFIR) filter with a minimum distance constraint (MDC). The filter is used to improve the dual-foot mounted pedestrian navigation. First, a corresponding navigation scheme is developed, in which two foot mounted IMUs are used to determine the human location. Next, the expectation maximization (EM)-based Kalman filter (KF) is used to estimate the noise covariances and optimize the averaging horizon for the UFIR filter. Finally, the MDC method is used to constrain the IMU cumulative errors. The performance of the proposed algorithm is verified experimentally using tests of 160 and 350 meters in length, and is compared with the existing dual-foot constraint and robust filtering works. It is shown that the MDconstrained and EM-assisted UFIR filter is effective in reducing the INS position error.

title: Sensorless Control of LC-Filtered PMSM Drives With An Easy-Tuning Adaptive Full-Order Observer
authors: Chao Li, Shaobin Li, Yongxiang Xu, Jibin Zou, Wentao Zhang, Shuo Chen, Zhixun Ma, Xiang Wu
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: In permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) drives, an LC filter helps sinusoidalize the motor terminal voltage, improving operational longevity. However, its inclusion increases model complexity, requiring a high-dimensional gain matrix for observability and complicating observer design. To overcome this issue, a novel adaptive full-order observer (AFO) is proposed for estimating stator current, stator voltage, extended back electromotive forces (EEMFs), rotor position, and speed in LC-filtered PMSM drives across medium- to high-speed ranges. Based on a frequency-domain dynamic error model, the introduced pole-placement method achieves stable pole assignment with only one tuning parameter. By formulating an error function that captures the effect of speed estimation error on pole placement, the unstable region of the system is effectively reduced through proper observer design. An improved speed adaptation law based on the gradient descent algorithm is also presented, with its stability rigorously proven via Lyapunov analysis in accordance with LaSalle's invariance principle. The resulting speed adaptation loop uses only one tuning parameter, ensuring asymptotic stability and ease of implementation. Furthermore, the influence of parameter variations on estimated states is analytically expressed, allowing quantitative error analysis and robustness assessment. Experimental results validate the proposed AFO design and parameter sensitivity analysis for sensorless control of LC-filtered PMSM drives.

title: Research on Temperature Error Compensation of Fiber Optic Gyroscope Based on Viscoelastic Dynamic Mechanism
authors: Qiongnan Shi, Jiajun Ma, Zhuo Zhang, Bin Long, Shan Xiao
abstract: Under complex varying-temperature conditions, fiber optic gyroscopes are prone to zero-bias drift, a major factor limiting their measurement accuracy. The traditional Shupe model considers only the instantaneous temperature gradient and fails to account for thermal hysteresis. Furthermore, most existing models predominantly employ linear elasticity assumptions, neglecting the viscoelasticity and stress relaxation characteristics of the fiber coil composite. This leads to insufficient compensation accuracy under dynamic temperature changes. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a thermo-mechanically coupled dynamic error compensation method based on the standard linear solid model. It innovatively integrates viscoelastic dynamics with the thermal hysteresis effect of FOGs to establish a physics-based model. By constructing a state-space model that incorporates temperature field distribution and stress relaxation mechanisms, and by introducing relaxation time constants to quantitatively characterize material memory effects, the proposed method effectively overcomes the oversimplified material assumptions in traditional models. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively eliminates thermal hysteresis effects, achieving notable compensation performance. The accuracy of the FOG-98 under temperature variations improved from 0.065°/h to 0.025°/h, representing an enhancement of 61.5%. These results verify the method's effectiveness and superiority in complex temperature environments.

title: Multi-domain Feature Integration based Trusted Partial Multi-view Incomplete Multi-label Learning
authors: Jie Wen, Jiaying Zhou, Xiaohuan Lu, Youliang Tian, Zheng Zhang, Li Shen, Yong Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Partial Multi-view Incomplete Multi-label Learning (PMvIMlL) focuses on the challenging multi-view multi-label learning tasks characterized by partially missing views and labels in real-world data. Although many methods have been proposed in recent years, most methods suffer from three distinct yet critical limitations. First, they lack effective mechanisms to quantify predictive uncertainty under dual missing settings, often yielding overconfident and unreliable predictions. Second, existing representations rely exclusively on spatial-domain features, overlooking the rich complementary information available in the frequency domain, which substantially limits their discriminative power. Third, prevailing fusion approaches fail to account for cross-view opinion divergence, frequently resulting in suboptimal fusion quality when handling discordant perspectives. To address these issues, we propose MFIT, a Multi-domain Feature Integration based Trusted Partial Multi-view Incomplete Multi-label Learning framework. Our method comprises two core components: a Feature Enhancement module via Frequency-Domain Integration (FE-FDI) and an Evidential Fusion module with Cross-view Decision Consistency (EF-CDC). FE-FDI enriches representations by incorporating global frequency patterns while preserving spatial characteristics. EF-CDC addresses the sensitivity issue of multi-view decision conflicts in the fusion process by enhancing cross-view decision consistency. Comprehensive evaluations on six datasets demonstrate that MFIT achieves substantial and consistent performance improvements, validating its effectiveness in enhancing both prediction accuracy and decision reliability.

title: PAPR Reduction in AFDM via Pre-Chirp Selection Assisted Active Constellation Extension and Pilot Position Optimization
authors: Anjie Zhang, Qi Zheng, Qiang Li, Yao Ge, Miaowen Wen
abstract: The recently proposed affine frequency division multiplexing (AFDM), which is based on multi-chirp waveforms, has demonstrated reliability and robustness in doubly selective fading channels. However, similar to other multi-carrier schemes, AFDM suffers from high peak-to-average power ratio (PAPR), which may cause signal distortion in power amplifier and performance degradation. To address this issue, this paper proposes, for the first time, an optimal pilot positioning (OPP) algorithm for pilot frames and a selective pre-chirp parameter assisted active constellation extension (SP-ACE) algorithm for subsequent data frames of AFDM systems. OPP selects the signal with the lowest PAPR by adjusting pilot symbol positions, while SP-ACE does so by tuning pre-chirp parameters and constellation points. Simulation results demonstrate that both algorithms achieve superior PAPR performance without sacrificing the bit error rate (BER) performance in the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) region of practical interest, and do not require the transmission of additional side information.

title: Research Status and Development Trends of Laser-Assisted Simulation of Dose-Rate Effects
authors: Ge Tang, Qiqi Liu, Jingrui Liu, Yao Xiao, Fuwang Zhang, Zhenkang Zhang, Xueying Wang, Shaobo Si, Yu Zhang, Suhan Tang, Mo Li
abstract: Pulsed-laser simulation provides a repeatable, safe, and effective complementary approach to large radiation facilities for studying transient ionizing dose-rate effects in semiconductor devices and integrated circuits. This review traces the development of laser-assisted test facilities over the past five decades and summarizes how wavelength selection, pulse-width control, beam/spot conditioning, and coordinated laser and X-ray/γ-ray platforms have expanded accessible test conditions. We discuss the physical basis of laser-radiation correspondence, from carrier generation and transport to circuit-level photocurrent phenomena, including rail-span collapse, and identify three dominant accuracy drivers: device/circuit structure and biasing, laser parameters, and temperature/thermal effects. Three conversion factor (CF) extraction approaches—carrier generation rate, peak photocurrent equivalence, and charge collection equivalence—are compared in terms of assumptions, uncertainty, and validity range. Among them, charge collection equivalence remains applicable in saturation regimes where peak-based metrics fail. Looking ahead, further progress in laser-assisted dose-rate simulation will depend on broader-band laser sources, such as UV-capable wide bandgap laser-assisted simulation systems, more reproducible CF establishment workflows, and AI-assisted electrical response modeling (e.g., technology computer aided design (TCAD) and finite-element analysis) for high-dimensional parameter studies.

title: An Ultra-wideband Miniaturized Sinusoidal Microwave Photonic Receiving Antenna for Optical Frequency Comb Undersampling Receiving Systems
authors: Yuhang Song, Yumo Tian, Shuguo Xie, Yan Yang, Rongguang Feng
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This letter presents a miniaturized sinusoidal microwave photonic receiving antenna (Sin-MPRA) for optical frequency comb (OFC) undersampling receivers operating from 1 to 18 GHz. Through the parallel configuration of the radiating structure and loading with an absorbing cavity, along with the embedding of a thin-film lithium niobate optical electric-field sensor (OES) featuring an exponentially tapered electrode, the antenna impedance is sufficiently tuned to directly achieve optimal voltage division, thereby eliminating the need for a high-profile, standalone impedance transformer. Capacitive edge loading enhances low-frequency response, while optimized copper-wire feed ensure near-lossless voltage transfer. The prototype achieves an equivalent system gain exceeding 0 dBi across the band, with a peak of 13.34 dBi, despite its compact size of 0.24 λ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">L</sub> in diameter and 0.04 λ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">L</sub> in height. Multi-signal reception in an OFC undersampling system validates its potential for wideband spectrum sensing on space-constrained platforms.

title: Drones in Automated and Robotized Internal Logistics: a Review of Optimization and Control Techniques
authors: Mojtaba Porghoveh, Raffaele Carli, Mariagrazia Dotoli
abstract: Drones have emerged as powerful tools for automation, real-time monitoring, and data collection, and their use in logistics continues to expand. In internal logistics, covering warehousing, inventory management, and intra-logistics tasks, drones offer opportunities to improve operational efficiency, accuracy, and cost-effectiveness. Despite these advantages, deploying drones in enclosed and cluttered indoor environments without GPS requires reliable control and mission-level optimization strategies. This paper reviews recent approaches to drone mission control and optimization for internal logistics applications over the past decade. Drone applications are organized into three categories: inventory management, surveillance and inspection, and intra-logistics operations. For each category, we discuss key challenges spanning mission-level decision problems such as task allocation and path planning, and execution-level requirements such as navigation, obstacle avoidance, and flight control. We compare classical, robust, and adaptive control methods with emerging learning-based approaches, highlighting their advantages, limitations, and implementation challenges. Finally, we identify open issues and outline research directions related to swarm coordination and human–drone interaction, which are important for scalable deployment in modern internal logistics environments.

title: Machine Learning-Based Computer Network Security Intrusion Detection and Prevention System
authors: Yanhong She
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 6th International Conference on Computer Network Security and Software Engineering
abstract: None
